howItWorks.intro: This lens filter size calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: What does an ND filter do?
faq.answer: An ND (neutral density) filter reduces the amount of light entering the lens without affecting color. This lets you use slower shutter speeds in bright conditions for effects like smooth water, cloud streaks, or motion blur.

faq.question: What ND filter strength should I get?
faq.answer: ND64 (6-stop) is the most versatile for general long exposure work. ND1000 (10-stop) is great for multi-second daytime exposures. A variable ND (2-8 stops) offers flexibility but may vignette on wide lenses.

faq.question: Do I need a specific filter size?
faq.answer: Filters must match your lens diameter (marked on the lens, e.g., 77mm). Common sizes: 52mm, 58mm, 67mm, 72mm, 77mm, 82mm. Buy for your largest lens and use step-down rings for smaller lenses.

faq.question: Is the Lens Filter Size Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard photography formulas trusted by photographers. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

sections.title: What Is the Lens Filter Size Calculator?
sections.body: The Lens Filter Size Calculator is a free online tool designed for photographers who need quick, accurate calculations in the photography space. By entering your base shutter speed, nd filter strength, aperture, you get instant results including new shutter speed, filter factor, light reduction. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting new shutter speed right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In photography, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by photographers worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to capture better images with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your base shutter speed and need to find the right new shutter speed. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Base Shutter Speed (no filter)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your base shutter speed — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: nd filter strength, aperture, iso. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your new shutter speed, filter factor, light reduction. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your base shutter speed before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
